title: invite code: 8 chars of Crockford Base32 from ULID random suffix is ~39 bits
description: jmap.rs space_invite_set generates the invite code as Ulid::new().to_string()[10..18].to_uppercase() — 8 characters of Crockford Base32 from the random suffix. Crockford Base32 uses 5 bits/char, so 8 chars = 40 bits. However the ULID random component is 80 bits split across chars [10..26] (16 chars). The slice [10..18] takes exactly the first 8 of those 16 chars = 40 bits entropy. This is borderline acceptable for a short-lived invite code, but with no rate limiting on Space/join (nie-0j6b.7), an attacker can brute-force 2^40 codes (1 trillion) offline if they know the code space is 8 Crockford chars. A 16-char code from the full random suffix (80 bits) would cost nothing extra and eliminate this concern.
